proposal_text:
Proposal number: 1333885

Proposal: LET'S WALK! - climate change awareness day event

Summary


Purpose: helping to rise the climate change awareness our world so urgently needs. 

Suggested actions: During that day we encourage people to walk and to:

leave their cars at home,
skip the bus,
avoid the subway and
turn to their own bodies in order to move around the city, by walking, by using their bicycles, their rollers, their skates.
If you are not lucky enough to be living near your work space you can still collaborate by doing home office and other activities such as walking to the grocery or the club.
Using a smartphone app we intend to track how much people walk during the day of the event. This information will then be published by social media and traditional media. The data provided by the app will also be translate in the reductions already mentioned, calculating savings in relationship  with the kilometrers walked and avoiding using transportation during that day.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page [14].
Because of the altruistic spirit of the cause, we expect to have support from the local ONG´s, health organizations and the government.     
Main value generation loops v1.0.png


Short term Impacts: we expect to save energy and money, GHG emissions, and we will contribute to build sustainable habits, for a healthy environment in the war against the dangers of a sedentary lifestyle.  

Long term impacts:

Since the proposal is to make people aware of how important it is to care about the planet. More climate change aware people would naturally support the following initiatives:

Recycling: We forecast that it could increase by 10 %,
 
Energy savings: recommendations use increment by 10 %
 
Distributed solar photovoltaic electricity law: is approved and covers 5 % of city demand.
 
Electric buses: their use is implemented in 2 % of city bus lines.
Is this proposal for a practice or a project?
Practice

What actions do you propose?
a) The main thrust of “Let’s Walk” is walking, because Buenos Aires has a high population density, and distances might be shorter than in other cities, so walking to work is a real possibility for some people. People can

Walk 10 - 15 blocks to their offices or back home.

Use public transportation, including buses, underground / subways and then walk the rest of the way to their offices or homes.

 

b) Cycling also adds value to the “Let’s walk” proposal for the population 10 to 30 blocks from their office. The Buenos Aires City already has implemented an “ecobici” (eco bikes) program [15]. The ecobici system has bikes parked in several distributed city points, and anyone can use one of these bikes for free using his / her document. You just return the bike in another bike parking place near your destination.

 

REVIEW OF SOME “BIKE TO WORK” PROGRAMS IN CANADA AND USA:

USA Today informs that Bike to Work programs might be rising in US [21].

Through a google search we found the following relevant links:

table-bike-to-work-projects.png


ANSWERS TO SOME OBJECTIONS TO “BIKE TO WORK”

The Huffpost article [16] mentions 9 objections to “Bike to work”, here are our thoughts about them!

main-objections.png

“LET’S WALK BUSINESS MODEL CANVAS”

LET`S WALK PROJECT BUSINESS CANVAS v2.0.png

As we know we have different partners in this event, which play a fundamental role.

On one side, the people enrolled in the event, that will participate registering using a mobile app. They will either walk to their works or commute by bike. In order to be eligible to receive a prize, each participant will allow the app to register information about distances walked / biked, which transportation they usually use to commute in order to calculate GHG emission savings due to the event. Those people that contribute with the biggest GHG emission savings will win prizes as well as  benefits from companies that sponsor the event.

It´s necessary to distinguish, that by incentivizing the movement we are preventing obesity, decrease bad cholesterol and increase good cholesterol, improving the state of mind and incorporating healthy habits, so typically HMOs and the city government will be very much interested, as in Argentina poor people use public hospitals maintained by city taxes.

On the other side, we need to be able to organize this event with which we need the Government's support to convey the event to the society. It will be benefited by an increased use of the public transport by the population, better flow of the traffic (less cars in the streets)  and at the same time it will reaffirm it´s commitment with the environment.

In addition to this, it will be a great opportunity to advertise the use of the eco-bikes(“ecobicis”) [15] and, as a result, the bike lanes.

On the other hand, the information generated by the app (big data)  will be of great utility since it will show which are the population's needs (for example: the best locations for entrances of subways , routes of buses or even which are the areas that are disconnected and need public transport).

It is also important to have the companies that will join on this great event allowing the use of “home office”. In addition, they will collaborate as sponsors and obtain in exchange Big Data to improve its business and find niche.

Sponsorships are one of the best ways of promoting a business and winning popularity between the target and generate good marketing for a brand. It can be specially used to increase credibility, image and prestige of the brand.

All participation is voluntary, and public transportation will still be available to those who want to use it.

LGC Team will ask for suggestions and advice from NGOs(such as WWF, who implemented "The earth hour"). In order to have more tools to guide the team on how to develop the project. 
 
Once LCG Team recieves feedback from NGOs.They will present the formal proposal to the Institute of Technology of Buenos Aires (ITBA) and to the MIT Colab
 
The team is looking forward to receive feedback from CLIMATE COLAB in order to improve the project. ITBA will give their consent and support to it.
 
We received the recognition and prize from MIT Colab thanks to the backing through votes from ITBA.
 
LGC Team will contact several organizations to help with fundraising and to add value to the final event (GOOGLE, MASS MEDIA, SPONSORS, etc). Nevertheless to make it happen we need firstly the GOVERNMENT authorization.
 
The government will notificate the companies of the proposal and ask for their cooperation by allowing the employees to work from home. They will also have to arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes.
 
Distance walked and GHG savings: To have an estimation of the GHGs saved is necessary an app (develop by GOOGLE if possible) that will measure them.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page.But it is important to keep record of the route like Google Maps does. 
 
Once the proposal is accepted the next step is to settle a date and communicate it to the society, a key actor in this process is MASS MEDIA. 
 
The day of the event SPONSORS will play an important role by motivating the population to walk more. This could be achived by offering refreshments and prizes. 
 
Finally,all the information is collected by GOOGLE. Including steps per person and the equivalence with the reduction of GHGs obteined.
 
The LGC Team will analyze the results and make papers to be published and to communicate it to the community, the government and the MIT. 
 
As the results will be positive this idea can be implemented in other countries, all over the world. By this the proposal will reach a greater impact and a change in people's habits worldwide.


Who will take these actions?
LGC Team at ITBA: designs proposal and presents it to Climate Colab.
 
Argentine Government sponsors "LET'S WALK day"
 
Media covers the events publishing metrics on energy saved, emissions saved and money saved.
 
5 % local city population participates in the "LET'S WALK day"
 
NGOs sponsor event.
 
Transportation works as usual.

Other countries can implement the proposal to have a higher impact
Where will these actions be taken?
City level: The first objective is to do it in Buenos Aires City as is the most populated region, the most contaminating one and the principal concentration of companies.

Country level: Later by the positive results our goal is to carry out the experience at country level, in cities like Cordoba, Rosario, Mendoza and San Luis.

Worldwide: Finally, other countries can use the proposal to make their own impacts since everything counts.

 

In addition, specify the country or countries where these actions will be taken.
Argentina

Country 2
Brazil

Country 3
Chile

Country 4
United States

Country 5
No country selected

Impact/Benefits
What impact will these actions have on greenhouse gas emissions and/or adapting to climate change?
1) City of Buenos Aires

a) Short term CO2e emissions saved in Buenos Aires city:

We postulate that 5 % of the population will participate in the event.
Public transportation use: 80% of population [1]

 
Emissions saved by cars commuting from Buenos Aires Province:

10410352 cars/month [2] /30 days/month x 15 km/trip  x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] x 0.05 ( 5 % population participates  in event) = 26 tCO2e (metric tons) / day.
 

Emission saved by cars inside the city:

2.89 million people x 5% people participation x 20 % people use cars x 7 km average trip to work x 2 trips /day x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] = 40.46 tCO2e / day
 

Total short term CO2e savings = (26 + 40.46) = 66.46 tCO2e /day

b) Long term CHG savings (5 years) in Buenos Aires city:

We forecast that recycling could increase by 10 %. Today only 23% of Buenos Aires city recycle [4]. And recycling saves from 0.34 to 10 Tonnes of CO2e from each ton of recycled material [5]. Considering an average 1 Tonne of CO2e saved per Tonne of recycled material, current Buenos Aires savings from recycling would be 976 Tonnes of domiciliary residues [6] x 23% people recycle x 0.1 (10 % recycling increase ) x 1 tCO2e/1 t recycled material = 22 Tonnes of CO2e / year saved.
 
Energy savings recommendations [7] use increment by 10 %. The average electricity consumption per electrified household in Argentina is 3600 kWh [11], so we estimate that the savings will be of 360 kWh/home per year * 963 thousand homes * 0.05 (5 % of participation) = 17334 kWh per year. This could save up to 17334 kWh x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 10.2 tCO2e/year
Distributed solar photo voltaic electricity law is approved and covers 1 % of city demand.
2.89 million people x 4100 kWh/person [10] x 0.01 (1% of demand) x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 70205 tCO2e/year
 
It incentivates the use of public transport in this way, so the Government must make it more efficient.For example, the use of electric buses is implemented in 2 % of city.[8] [9] Each bus will save 175 metrics tones of pollutant emissions and 22.700 liters of fuel per unit and year. We estimate that there are 10200 buses in Buenos Aires,so 2% is 204, the total reduction will be 204*175 mtones= 35700 metric tones of pollutant / year saved.[10]
 
Total emission savings = (22 + 10.2 + 70205 + 35700) = 105937 tCO2e/year

2) Extrapolation to world:

a) Short term:

66.46 tCO2e/(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 24997 tCO2e

b) Long term:

105.937 tCO2e/year /(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 39 million tCO2e / year.

The transportation sector accounts from 19 % to 25 % of total GHGs emissions, according to [12] and [13]

So the total GHG due to the transportation sector may be calculated as

46.000 million tCO2e x 22% = 10.120 million tCO2e

So the proposal savings would be 39 / 10120 = 0.38 % of the transportation sector emissions.

What are other key benefits?
Generate awareness on using more public transport
Government will have to improve the efficiency of public transport
Change the habits of the society towards a sustainable and ecological life
Smog and sound pollution will be reduced
People´s stress levels will be reduced
Increase the use of bicycles
Change habits and weaken sedentary lifestyle
Cut costs of transport and so combustibles
By implemeting home-office besides helping this cause, the employeers will have a cut down of their costs
Decrease GHGs emissions by reducing the use of cars and public transport
Costs/Challenges
What are the proposal’s projected costs?
1) Cost of security forces involved in organizing the event.

2) Cost of health support personnel, ambulances, paramedics,

3) Cost of traditional media ads

4) Cost of blog, SN ads and  social media managers / influencers

5) Cost of winner prizes

6) Cost of collaboration agreement with smartphone apps that calculate distances walked by people participating in the event

7) Cost of webpage that summarizes energy savings, money saved, GHGs emission saved.

Timeline
The day of the event, there will be an immediate impact by the reduction of the GHGs emissions.
In the short-term we are looking forward to make people realize that their can make a huge change on a daily basis.For example, they can avoid the use of cars in short distances.
Over more, we can affirm that one of important change will be the use bikes. Nowadays, Buenos Aires city provides free bikes and bicicles in the downtown. Our idea is to massify these kind of habits and became a green country free of sedentarism.
What's more, we want to Incentive companies to stablish a home-office day. This will allow people who live far from their jobs to colaborate to this cause.
Medium-term: These "Homeoffice days" could at first be once a year, but once people catch the habit, it could be done monthly.
Long-term: Eventually people could do these "Homeoffice days" weekly, so that people enjoy life a little more and savings of energy, money and GHGs emissions are multiplied.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'perfect storm' of unverified assumptions: 5% of the city's population voluntarily walking/biking to work on a single day, simultaneous government authorization for mass home-office adoption, and the development of a custom tracking app by a major tech giant (Google) without a defined technical or contractual pathway. The causal chain assumes that a one-day behavioral nudge will trigger complex systemic changes (e.g., 2% of bus fleet electrification, 1% solar coverage) which are policy-dependent, not event-dependent.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day—is a well-established global practice (e.g., Bike to Work Day in the US/Canada, referenced in the text). The proposal adds a smartphone tracking component, but the underlying mechanism is not novel. The novelty is further diluted by the reliance on existing 'ecobici' infrastructure and standard ecological footprint algorithms.

impact.rationale: The quantitative impact claims are scientifically unsound due to severe logical fallacies. The proposal extrapolates a one-day event's direct emissions savings (66 tCO2e) to a global annual figure (39 million tCO2e) by assuming the event's participation rate and behavioral changes scale linearly to the entire global population and persist year-round. Furthermore, it attributes long-term policy outcomes (solar law approval, bus electrification) directly to the event, ignoring the multi-year legislative and capital investment cycles required for such infrastructure. The causal link between a single awareness day and a 10% increase in recycling or 2% bus fleet electrification is not supported by empirical evidence.

presentation.rationale: The presentation suffers from significant technical and logical inconsistencies. The text contains typos ('kilometrers', 'aligorithm'), contradictory statements (advocating to 'skip the bus' in the summary but later stating 'public transportation use: 80%' and 'incentivates the use of public transport'), and a lack of rigorous methodology in the impact calculations. The reliance on 'Big Data' from a hypothetical Google app without a data privacy or technical architecture discussion undermines the scientific credibility.

A1.perspective_summary: From a systems and empirical evidence perspective, this proposal conflates a short-term awareness campaign with long-term structural policy changes. While the intent to promote active transport is sound, the causal mechanisms proposed to achieve the stated long-term impacts (solar adoption, bus electrification) are tenuous. The quantitative modeling is fundamentally flawed, treating a one-day behavioral anomaly as a scalable, permanent global constant. The proposal lacks the technical and policy rigor required to support its ambitious impact claims.
key_concerns: Causal Fallacy: The proposal assumes a single-day event can directly cause multi-year policy shifts (e.g., solar law approval, bus fleet electrification) without detailing the intermediate political or economic mechanisms.

Extrapolation Error: The global impact calculation (39 million tCO2e/year) incorrectly scales a one-day local event to a global annual figure, ignoring population density, infrastructure availability, and behavioral persistence.

Implementation Gap: The proposal assumes the immediate availability of a custom Google-developed app and government-mandated home-office days without addressing the technical, legal, or bureaucratic hurdles.

Contradictory Messaging: The summary explicitly advises to 'skip the bus' and 'avoid the subway,' while the impact section claims the event will 'incentivize the use of public transport,' creating a logical inconsistency in the behavioral model.

Data Validity: The reliance on a hypothetical 'Big Data' output from an unproven app to drive policy decisions (e.g., subway entrance locations) is speculative and lacks a defined data validation protocol.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'magic wand' assumption that the government will unilaterally mandate home office, re-route public transport, and authorize a city-wide event without a detailed implementation plan. The governance gap is critical: the text states the government 'will have to arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes' and 'notify companies,' yet offers no mechanism for enforcement or negotiation. Furthermore, the reliance on a Google-developed app and the assumption that 5% of the population will voluntarily participate in a single-day event without a robust mobilization strategy renders the operational feasibility low. The proposal conflates a one-off awareness day with systemic policy change, ignoring the institutional inertia required to shift commuting habits.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day—is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day in the US/Canada, which the proposal itself cites). The proposal adds little novelty beyond the specific context of Buenos Aires. The 'big data' component is framed as a novel value add, but the proposal admits to using existing algorithms and generic tracking methods, lacking a unique governance model or innovative data-sharing framework that distinguishes it from standard corporate or municipal CSR initiatives.

impact.rationale: The quantitative impact claims are mathematically unsound and logically flawed. The proposal extrapolates a single-day event's short-term savings (66 tCO2e) into long-term annual savings (105,937 tCO2e) by assuming a 10% increase in recycling and a 1% coverage of solar PV law *resulting* from a walking event. This is a non-sequitur; a one-day walk does not causally drive the passage of solar laws or the adoption of electric buses. The long-term impact model assumes that 'awareness' automatically translates into specific legislative and infrastructural outcomes (e.g., 'Distributed solar photovoltaic electricity law: is approved'), which ignores the complex political economy of policy adoption. The actual impact is likely limited to the short-term behavioral nudge of the event day itself.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with standard sections (Business Model Canvas, Impact/Benefits), but the internal logic is inconsistent. It oscillates between a 'practice' and a 'project' without clarity. The presentation of data is confusing, mixing short-term event metrics with long-term policy forecasts as if they are direct outcomes of the event. While the intent is clear, the lack of a coherent governance narrative—specifically how the 'LGC Team' transitions from a student group to a policy influencer capable of moving government and corporate actors—weakens the professional presentation of the plan.

A2.perspective_summary: From a governance and regulatory standpoint, this proposal is a classic case of 'awareness over action.' It assumes that a single event can trigger a cascade of complex policy changes (solar laws, bus electrification) without addressing the institutional levers, political constraints, or regulatory frameworks required to make those changes happen. The feasibility is low because it treats government and corporate actors as passive recipients of a proposal rather than active stakeholders with their own agendas. The impact calculations are fundamentally flawed, conflating correlation with causation and ignoring the time-lag and political friction inherent in policy implementation.
key_concerns: The proposal assumes the government will unilaterally mandate home office and re-route public transport without a negotiation strategy or legal basis.

The long-term impact model is logically invalid: it attributes the passage of solar laws and the adoption of electric buses to a one-day walking event, ignoring the separate political processes required for these outcomes.
There is no clear accountability mechanism: Who is responsible if the 'big data' reveals infrastructure gaps? How does the government act on this data without a formal mandate?
The reliance on a 'Google-developed app' is a single point of failure; there is no contingency for data privacy, app development costs, or user adoption rates.
The proposal fails to address the 'free rider' problem and the difficulty of sustaining behavior change beyond a single event day.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'magic bullet' assumption that government and corporate entities will instantly restructure operations (home office mandates, route modifications) based on a single event. The operational complexity of coordinating security, health support, and real-time data tracking across a city of 3 million people for a one-off event is underestimated. Furthermore, the reliance on a custom Google-developed app and the assumption that 5% of the population will voluntarily participate without significant friction or safety concerns in a dense urban environment like Buenos Aires presents a high execution risk.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days—is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day in the US/Canada, which the proposal itself cites). The proposal adds little new operational methodology, merely repackaging existing awareness campaigns with a specific data-tracking layer that is technically unproven in this context. The 'novelty' is superficial, relying on the aggregation of existing ideas rather than a new mechanism for behavior change.

impact.rationale: The quantitative impact claims are mathematically flawed and operationally unrealistic. Extrapolating a one-day event's savings to a 0.38% reduction in global transportation emissions (39 million tCO2e/year) is a gross logical error; a single day of behavior change cannot generate annualized structural shifts without a permanent policy change, which the proposal does not guarantee. The long-term impact relies on a 'trickle-down' theory of awareness (i.e., walking one day leads to 10% more recycling and 2% electric bus adoption) that lacks empirical evidence and ignores the inertia of infrastructure and policy.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with standard sections (Business Model Canvas, Impact calculations), but the internal logic is inconsistent. The text oscillates between a 'practice' and a 'project,' and the financial modeling is absent despite listing cost categories. The presentation of data is confusing, mixing short-term event metrics with long-term policy outcomes as if they are direct causal results of the event. While the intent is clear, the professional rigor required for implementation planning is missing.

A3.perspective_summary: From an implementation standpoint, this proposal is a classic 'awareness event' masquerading as a scalable climate solution. It fails to address the critical path of *sustained* behavior change, relying instead on a one-day spike in activity that will likely revert immediately after the event. The operational burden on the government and the lack of a concrete, funded business model for the app and logistics make it highly risky to execute as described. The impact projections are mathematically unsound, conflating a single day's data with multi-year structural transformation.
key_concerns: The assumption that a one-day event can drive long-term policy changes (e.g., electric bus adoption, solar laws) is a critical logical fallacy; correlation does not equal causation.
No clear funding mechanism is identified for the app development, security, and prizes; the proposal assumes 'altruistic spirit' will cover operational costs.
The '5% participation' target is arbitrary and lacks a recruitment strategy; in a dense city, safety and traffic management for 145,000+ walkers on a single day is a massive logistical hurdle.
The proposal assumes Google will develop a custom app for free; this is a high-risk dependency with no fallback plan.
The extrapolation of one-day savings to annual global impact (39 million tCO2e) is mathematically invalid and undermines the credibility of the entire impact assessment.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'magic bullet' assumption that the government will mandate home office and re-route public transport for a single day, and that Google will build a custom app for free. From the ground, the friction of coordinating thousands of employers, transit authorities, and tech giants for a one-off event is immense. Furthermore, the calculation that 5% of the population will walk 10-15km (or bike 30km) in a single day ignores physical reality, weather, and the fact that many workers live in the Greater Buenos Aires conurbation where distances are too great for walking, making the participation target unrealistic without coercion.

novelty.rationale: The concept of 'Walk to Work' days and 'Bike to Work' challenges is well-established globally (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day). The proposal adds a smartphone tracking layer, but this is a standard feature in existing fitness apps (Strava, Google Fit) rather than a novel innovation. The 'novelty' of using big data to plan transit is a byproduct of existing mobility apps, not a new mechanism invented here.

impact.rationale: The projected impact is mathematically inflated and disconnected from lived reality. The proposal claims a 39 million tonne annual reduction globally based on a single day's event, which is a logical fallacy (extrapolating a one-day anomaly to a year). More critically, the proposal explicitly asks people to 'skip the bus' and 'avoid the subway.' For low-income residents in Buenos Aires who rely on public transit for survival, this is not a sustainable alternative; it forces them to walk excessive distances or stay home, potentially excluding the very populations most affected by climate change and pollution. The 'impact' on GHG is negligible compared to the disruption caused to daily life.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but the tone is overly optimistic and detached from the complexities of urban mobility. It presents a 'business model' that assumes voluntary compliance from major corporations and government bodies without addressing the logistical nightmares of implementation. The reliance on 'altruistic spirit' as a primary driver for government and corporate action is a weak argument in a professional proposal context.

A4.perspective_summary: This proposal treats a complex urban mobility system as a simple behavioral switch, ignoring the structural barriers that prevent people from walking or biking. By asking people to 'skip the bus,' it inadvertently penalizes those who cannot walk long distances, creating a class-based exclusion. The massive impact projections are based on flawed extrapolations of a one-day event, masking the fact that the actual, sustainable impact of a single 'Walk Day' is likely minimal and temporary.
key_concerns: The instruction to 'skip the bus' and 'avoid the subway' is dangerous and exclusionary for low-income workers who cannot walk 10+ km to work; this creates a barrier to entry for the most vulnerable populations.

The assumption that 5% of the population (approx. 145,000 people) will walk or bike to work in a single day ignores distance constraints, safety concerns, and weather, making the participation target highly unrealistic.

The proposal assumes Google will develop a custom app and the government will re-route transit for free; this dependency on external, uncommitted actors makes the project non-feasible without a concrete, funded implementation plan.
The long-term impact calculations (39 million tonnes) are mathematically invalid, extrapolating a one-day event to a full year of global savings, which undermines the credibility of the proposal.
There is no plan for safety infrastructure (sidewalks, bike lanes) or security for pedestrians/cyclists, which is a critical gap in a city with high traffic density and safety concerns.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'magic wand' assumption that government and corporate actors will unilaterally restructure labor laws (mandatory home office) and transport routes based on a single-day event. The requirement for government authorization to 'modify routes' and 'ask for cooperation' without a detailed implementation plan or political leverage makes the operational feasibility critically low. Furthermore, the reliance on a Google-developed app and specific government algorithms introduces significant dependency risks that are not mitigated.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day—is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day). The proposal adds little normative or structural novelty, merely repackaging existing awareness campaigns with a specific app tracking mechanism. The 'business model' of selling big data to sponsors is a standard commercial practice, not an innovative solution to the climate crisis.

impact.rationale: The proposal commits a severe distributional error by extrapolating a one-day event's data to claim long-term structural changes (e.g., 10% recycling increase, 5% solar coverage, 2% electric bus implementation). These long-term impacts are causally disconnected from a single day of walking. The short-term impact (66 tCO2e) is negligible on a city scale, and the proposal fails to address the 'rebound effect' or the exclusion of those who cannot walk (low-income workers in peripheries, disabled individuals), rendering the net impact highly questionable.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections but suffers from internal contradictions (e.g., asking people to 'skip the bus' in the summary, then later encouraging public transport use). The mathematical extrapolations are presented with false precision, masking the lack of empirical evidence for the causal links between a one-day walk and multi-year policy shifts. The tone is optimistic but lacks the rigor required to justify the claimed scale of impact.

A5.perspective_summary: This proposal masks a lack of structural ambition behind a veneer of 'awareness.' It treats complex systemic issues (transport infrastructure, labor rights, energy grids) as if they can be solved by a single day of voluntary individual behavior change. The distributional consequences are ignored: the proposal assumes all citizens have the physical ability and geographic proximity to walk to work, effectively excluding the poor, the disabled, and those in sprawling peripheries. The claimed long-term impacts are speculative fantasies, not grounded in the causal reality of a one-day event.
key_concerns: Exclusion Risk: The proposal assumes universal physical ability and proximity to work, ignoring the structural barriers faced by low-income workers, the elderly, and the disabled who cannot walk or bike to work.
Causal Fallacy: The proposal falsely attributes long-term systemic changes (solar law approval, electric bus implementation) to a single-day awareness event, hiding the lack of a real policy pathway.

Data Privacy & Exploitation: The model relies on harvesting 'big data' from participants to sell to sponsors and governments, raising ethical concerns about the commodification of citizen movement without clear consent frameworks or data sovereignty.

False Neutrality on Transport: The proposal initially suggests 'skipping the bus' (which is often a low-carbon option) in favor of walking, potentially discouraging efficient public transit use in favor of individual effort, which is not always scalable or fair.

Implementation Gap: The proposal assumes government and corporate compliance (home office mandates, route changes) without addressing the political economy or labor negotiations required to make this happen.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'magic bullet' assumption where a single-day event triggers complex, multi-year systemic shifts (e.g., passing solar laws, deploying electric buses) without a defined mechanism for political or infrastructural change. The dependency on government authorization to modify bus routes and corporate mandates for home office, combined with the expectation that a one-off awareness day will force these structural changes, renders the long-term execution highly improbable.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day with app-based tracking—is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day). The proposal adds no novel systemic intervention or technological breakthrough; it merely repackages existing awareness campaigns with a specific focus on Buenos Aires, lacking a unique approach to the deeper drivers of urban mobility.

impact.rationale: The proposal conflates short-term behavioral nudges with long-term structural transformation. The claimed long-term impacts (e.g., 10% recycling increase, 5% solar coverage) are causally disconnected from a single day of walking; there is no evidence that a one-day event creates the political will or capital investment required for such massive infrastructure shifts. The 'impact' is likely a temporary reduction in emissions (66 tCO2e/day) that vanishes the next day, failing to build genuine resilience or shift the baseline trajectory of the city's carbon footprint.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts quantitative modeling, but the logic is internally inconsistent. It presents a 'Business Model Canvas' and 'value loops' for what is essentially a volunteer awareness campaign, and the mathematical extrapolation from a one-day event to global 39 million tCO2e/year savings is logically flawed and misleading. The presentation obscures the lack of a causal chain between the event and the massive long-term outcomes.

A6.perspective_summary: From a long-term sustainability lens, this proposal is a classic case of 'symbolic action' masquerading as systemic change. While the immediate act of walking is beneficial, the proposal's core failure is the assumption that a one-day awareness event can catalyze the deep structural transformations (legislation, infrastructure, energy grid shifts) required for genuine climate resilience. It risks creating a 'resilience illusion' where stakeholders feel they have acted, while the underlying high-carbon systems remain untouched.
key_concerns: Causal Fallacy: The proposal assumes a direct causal link between a one-day walking event and the approval of complex legislation (solar laws) and infrastructure deployment (electric buses) over 5 years, without explaining the political or economic mechanism for this shift.

Resilience vs. Shift: The proposal does not build systemic resilience; it merely shifts behavior for 24 hours. If the event does not lead to permanent infrastructure changes (e.g., safe bike lanes, reliable public transit), the carbon savings are negligible in the long run.

Data Integrity: The reliance on a smartphone app to track 'GHG savings' based on voluntary participation introduces significant data bias and does not account for the 'rebound effect' (e.g., people driving more on other days to compensate).

Scalability Flaw: The extrapolation of local short-term savings to global long-term impacts (39 million tCO2e/year) is mathematically unsound and ignores the diminishing returns and systemic barriers of scaling a one-off event globally.

Dependency Risk: The entire model hinges on government and corporate cooperation (home office mandates, route changes) which are unlikely to be granted based solely on the promise of a single awareness day.

conflicts.summary: A1 assigns a score of 2 to presentation, citing 'significant technical and logical inconsistencies' including typos and contradictory messaging ('skip the bus' vs. 'incentivize public transport'). In contrast, A2 through A6 all assign a score of 3, acknowledging structural issues but characterizing the presentation as 'structured with standard sections' or having 'clear sections' despite the logical flaws. The divergence lies in whether the internal contradictions and lack of rigor constitute a fundamental failure of presentation (A1) or merely a weakness within an otherwise standard format (A2-A6).

clarification_requests.question: You assigned a '2' for presentation due to 'significant technical and logical inconsistencies,' while the other five experts assigned a '3' despite acknowledging the same contradictions. Specifically, do you consider the contradiction between advising to 'skip the bus' and later claiming to 'incentivize public transport' as a fatal flaw that invalidates the entire document's professional credibility, or is it a correctable error that does not preclude a '3' score? Please clarify the threshold at which logical inconsistency transitions from a presentation weakness to a presentation failure.

clarification_responses.question: You assigned a '2' for presentation due to 'significant technical and logical inconsistencies,' while the other five experts assigned a '3' despite acknowledging the same contradictions. Specifically, do you consider the contradiction between advising to 'skip the bus' and later claiming to 'incentivize public transport' as a fatal flaw that invalidates the entire document's professional credibility, or is it a correctable error that does not preclude a '3' score? Please clarify the threshold at which logical inconsistency transitions from a presentation weakness to a presentation failure.
clarification_responses.answer:
The contradiction between advising participants to 'skip the bus' and 'avoid the subway' in the summary versus the later claim that the event 'incentivizes the use of public transport' is not merely a stylistic inconsistency; it represents a fundamental failure in the proposal's logical coherence and systems modeling. In a scientific evaluation, the 'Presentation' score reflects the clarity, rigor, and internal consistency of the argument. When a proposal explicitly advocates for the avoidance of a specific system component (public transit) while simultaneously claiming a positive outcome for that same system, it demonstrates a lack of causal understanding. 

My threshold for a 'Presentation Failure' (score < 3) is crossed when logical inconsistencies are not just typos but indicate a breakdown in the core hypothesis or mechanism. Here, the contradiction invalidates the behavioral model: if the primary action is to avoid transit, the claimed benefit of 'better flow' or 'incentivized use' cannot be causally derived from the proposed actions without a complex, unexplained mediation. This is not a correctable typo; it is a structural flaw in the narrative logic. While other experts may have viewed this as a minor editing error, from a systems perspective, it suggests the authors have not rigorously stress-tested their own causal chain. Therefore, I maintain the score of 2. The inconsistency is symptomatic of the broader 'Causal Fallacy' identified in the Impact and Feasibility scores, where the proposal conflates distinct mechanisms (avoidance vs. adoption) without defining the transition. A score of 3 would imply the document is professionally credible and logically sound enough to serve as a basis for further technical refinement, which this contradiction precludes.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'perfect storm' of unverified assumptions: a 5% voluntary participation rate in a single-day event, immediate government mandates for home-office and route modifications, and the free development of a custom tracking app by a major tech giant (Google). The causal chain assumes a one-day behavioral nudge will trigger complex, multi-year systemic changes (e.g., bus fleet electrification, solar law approval) without detailing the necessary political, economic, or legislative mechanisms. The operational complexity of coordinating security and logistics for 145,000+ participants in a dense urban environment is severely underestimated.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day—is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day in the US/Canada). The proposal adds a smartphone tracking component, but this relies on existing algorithms and generic tracking methods rather than a novel technological or governance innovation. The 'big data' value proposition is a standard feature of existing mobility apps, not a unique mechanism invented here.

impact.rationale: The quantitative impact claims are scientifically unsound due to severe logical fallacies. The proposal extrapolates a one-day event's direct emissions savings (66 tCO2e) to a global annual figure (39 million tCO2e) by assuming linear scaling and permanent behavioral persistence, which is mathematically invalid. Furthermore, it attributes long-term policy outcomes (solar law approval, bus electrification) directly to the event, ignoring the multi-year legislative and capital investment cycles required. The causal link between a single awareness day and a 10% increase in recycling or 2% bus fleet electrification is not supported by empirical evidence.

presentation.rationale: The presentation suffers from significant technical and logical inconsistencies that undermine professional credibility. A critical contradiction exists where the summary explicitly advises participants to 'skip the bus' and 'avoid the subway,' while the impact section claims the event will 'incentivize the use of public transport.' This is not a minor typo but a fundamental breakdown in the behavioral model and causal logic. Additionally, the text contains typos ('kilometrers', 'aligorithm') and presents mathematical extrapolations with false precision, masking the lack of empirical evidence for the claimed causal links.

final.synthesis:
The synthesis of expert judgments reveals a unanimous consensus on the low scores across all dimensions, driven by three core failures: logical inconsistency, mathematical invalidity, and operational naivety. 

1. **Presentation & Logic**: A key disagreement was resolved regarding the 'skip the bus' contradiction. While some experts initially viewed this as a correctable error (scoring 3), the rigorous analysis (A1) demonstrated that this contradiction invalidates the proposal's core behavioral hypothesis. If the action is to avoid transit, the claimed benefit of incentivizing transit is logically impossible without an unexplained mediation. This structural flaw in the narrative logic justifies the consensus score of 2, as the document fails to present a coherent causal chain.

2. **Impact & Causality**: All experts agreed that the impact calculations are fundamentally flawed. The proposal commits a severe extrapolation error, treating a one-day anomaly as a permanent global constant. The attribution of long-term policy shifts (solar laws, electric buses) to a single-day event was universally rejected as a 'causal fallacy.' The synthesis confirms that the short-term impact is negligible (66 tCO2e) and the long-term projections are speculative fantasies.

3. **Feasibility**: The consensus is that the proposal relies on 'magic wand' assumptions regarding government and corporate cooperation. The expectation that Google will build a custom app for free and that the government will unilaterally mandate home-office days without a negotiation strategy renders the project operationally unfeasible.

The final judgment reflects that while the *intent* to raise awareness is positive, the *mechanism* proposed is logically incoherent, mathematically unsound, and operationally impossible as described.
final.uncertainty: The primary remaining uncertainty lies in the potential for the proposal to be re-scoped as a purely symbolic awareness campaign rather than a driver of structural change. If the authors were to strip away the inflated long-term impact claims (solar laws, bus electrification) and the contradictory 'skip the bus' messaging, the feasibility of a one-day walk event might improve. However, as currently written, the proposal's reliance on unverified dependencies (Google app, government mandates) and its flawed causal logic leave no room for a higher score without a complete rewrite of the core hypothesis.
